summary:
The abolitionist movement in the United States was the organized campaign to end chattel slavery that grew from a small religious and Quaker-led effort in the late eighteenth century into a significant political and cultural force by the 1850s. It ultimately helped create the political conditions for the [[event:2537|abolition of slavery]] through the Thirteenth Amendment in 1865, though the movement's relationship with the Civil War and its aftermath was complex and contested.

American abolitionism took two main forms. Moral suasion abolitionists, led by William Lloyd Garrison (who founded the newspaper The Liberator in 1831 and the American Anti-Slavery Society in 1833), argued that slavery was a moral evil requiring immediate, unconditional abolition and that Christians were individually obligated to refuse complicity with it. Garrison famously refused to participate in electoral politics and burned copies of the Constitution as a 'covenant with death.' Political abolitionists, including the Liberty Party (1840) and later the Free Soil Party (1848), argued that opposing slavery's expansion through politics was more practical than demanding its immediate abolition.

The movement depended heavily on the testimony of formerly enslaved people. Frederick Douglass, who escaped slavery in 1838 and became the movement's greatest orator and writer, demonstrated through his very existence and eloquence the absurdity of arguments for Black inferiority. Harriet Tubman made perhaps nineteen trips back into the South to guide approximately 70 enslaved people to freedom through the Underground Railroad, a network of safe houses and routes to the North and Canada. Harriet Beecher Stowe's novel Uncle Tom's Cabin (1852), which sold 300,000 copies in its first year, made the human reality of slavery vivid for millions of Northerners who had never seen it.

The movement was internally divided over tactics, the role of women (many female abolitionists were also pioneers of the women's suffrage movement), and the question of violence. John Brown's violent raids, culminating in the raid on Harpers Ferry in 1859, polarized opinion; to Southern slaveholders he confirmed fears of a Northern conspiracy, while to many abolitionists he was a martyr.
